content.full_text:
STATE OF CALI FOR.NIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacrame nto, CA 9581 t 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Amo Harris 
104 Funston Ave 
San Francisco, CA 94118 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-17-237 
Dear Mr. Harris: 
October 31, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding post-governmental employment 
provisions of the Political Reform Act ( .. Act").1 Because your request is general in nature and does 
not involve a specific proceeding, we must treat your request as one for informal assistance.2 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I 
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Are the Act's revolving door restrictions applicable to your prospective employment 
activities following your service as a member on the California Citizens Oversight Board? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. You are subject to the Act's revolving door restrictions. 
FACTS 
On October 29, 2014, former State Attorney General Kamala Harris appointed you to the 
statutorily-created California Citizens Oversight Board. The Oversight Board consists of nine 
voting members and two ex-officio members. As a voting member, you held a designated position 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) Advice provided relates only to future acts; we make no 
comment as to any past acts of the public official. (Regulation I 8329(b)(8).) 


File No. 1-17-23 7 
Page No. 2 
in the Oversight Board's conflict-of-interest code. You concluded your service on the Oversight 
Board in August 2017. 
Proposition 39 (Nov. 2012 statewide general election), its enabling legislation, and 
subsequent legislation established the Clean Energy Jobs Act and related Clean Energy Job 
Creation Fund. The California Energy Commission ("CEC") oversaw the implementation of the 
Clean Energy Jobs Act. Proposition 39 and its related legislation further provided for the 
establishment of the Oversight Board to review expenditures, audit the Clean Energy Job Creation 
Fund, and maintain transparency and accountability of the fund. 
In addition to the Board's oversight duties, it provides an annual report to the Legislature 
summarizing the results of its work and makes recommendations to improve Clean Energy Jobs Act 
programs. According to you, the Oversight Board's recommendations were never adopted by the 
Legislature. 
The Board also provides high level, non-specific recommendations to the CEC about policy 
level improvements. You believe that "far less than half' of the Board's recommendations were 
adopted by the CEC to change the program. The Board never recommended specific language or 
specific policies. Instead, it made broad recommendations on policy direction that were taken into 
consideration but never incorporated into actual language. 
Members of the Oversight Board are appointed by the California Treasurer, Attorney 
General, and ControHer with two ex officio members from the CEC and the California Public 
Utilities Commission. The Board is staffed and supported by the CEC. However, you state that the 
Board has no formal decision-making authority. 
You do not have any specific plans to lobby or seek to influence any California state 
authorities or legislation at present. However, your work over the last 17 years has been in 
renewable energy, and you have lobbied or sought to influence state policy in the past. You are 
involved - as an investor and advisor- in renewable energy development, electric vehicle 
development, and energy efficiency projects. Those activities may entail lobbying or seeking to 
influence state energy, transportation, or other policies in the future. 
ANALYSIS 
For state officials, the Act has two post-governmental employment restrictions and a ban on 
influencing prospective employment, generally known as the "revolving door" provisions: 
(1) The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from communicating with his or 
her former agency to influence the agency's administrative or legislative action (Section 
87406); 
(2) The "permanent ban" bars a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating in any specific proceeding between two parties that the employee worked 
on while in state service (Sections 87400-87405); 
(3) The ban on influencing prospective employment prohibits a state employee from 
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision that directly 
relates to a perspective employer (Section 87407). 


File No. 1-17-237 
Page No. 3 
All state public officials are subject to the Act's revolving door provisions. A public official 
is every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state governmental agency. (Section 82048.) 
However, a member does not include an individual who perfonns duties as part of a committee, 
board, commission, group, or other body that does not have decisionmaking authority. (Regulation 
18700(c)(2).) Thus, the determinative question is whether the Citizen Oversight Committee has 
decisionmaking authority. 
While you have stated that the Citizen Oversight Committee "has no fonnal decision­
making authority," the Citizen Oversight Committee makes high level recommendations and has 
adopted a conflict of interest code. You are also a designated official under the code. A 
governmental official should be designated in his or her agency's conflict-of-interest code only if 
the official makes or participates in making governmental decisions that have a reasonably 
foreseeable material effect on any financial interest. (Section 87302.) Accordingly, even if the 
Citizen Oversight Committee is not the final decision maker for any specific decision, the 
Committee does appear to have decisionmaking authority, and you are subject to the Act's 
revolving door rules. If you need additional assistance regarding the revolving door provisions, you 
may wish to consult the Commission's fact sheet, Leaving State Employment, which is available at 
)Htp: I lww,w. time.ca. gov/contcnt/dam/ty,pc/N S-
Documcntsff AD/Pub I ic%20Officials%20and%20Emplovccst Lcavin!J State Emglo)1ncnt.od t: 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
RPOC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
7 
Ryan P. O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division

QUESTION: Are the Act's revolving door restrictions applicable to your prospective employment 
activities following your service as a member on the California Citizens Oversight Board?

CONCLUSION: Yes. You are subject to the Act's revolving door restrictions.
